text: The surgeon performs a lateral to medial dissection during a high anterior resection, dividing the lateral attachments of the sigmoid colon. The sigmoid and descending colon are being laterally mobilized.

The surgeon continues dissection of the descending colon's peritoneal attachments, using medial retraction to maintain tension and facilitate the process. The lateral dissection progresses towards the splenic flexure.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection to reveal the sidewall anatomy, with the extent of dissection adjustable based on the required length of the colon.

The surgeon retracts the colon laterally to facilitate medial dissection of the vessels. The peritoneum is divided up to the site of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), revealing lateral anatomical structures. Further dissection of the peritoneum around the vascular pedicle is performed.

The surgeon retracts the pedicle and confirms the position of the ureter. A surgical stapler is inserted around the pedicle, which is then divided, completing the separation of the pedicle from surrounding structures.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the abdominal side wall, connecting the medial and lateral planes. Further division of the medial peritoneal attachments is performed to increase the length of the mobilized colon.
The surgeon performs dissection of the upper and mid rectum within the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, releasing bilateral peritoneal attachments to facilitate rectal mobilization.

The surgeon skeletonizes the rectal tube to facilitate division, using a laparoscopic stapler for intracorporeal division of the rectum. The bowel is resected outside the abdomen, and the anvil is placed and returned to the abdominal cavity. The stapling spike is deployed, the anvil is connected, orientation is checked, and the stapling device is closed.
The surgeon performs an air leak test to check the integrity of the anastomosis, ensuring there are no visible leaks at the surgical site.
